metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback / emergency-restore procedure to WS7: at minimum, document the GitHub admin steps to temporarily re-enable branch-protection direct-push access (or a break-glass bypass label) so Gerrit downtime doesn't produce a merge freeze.
findings.finding: WS7 removes the legacy GitHub branch protection (making Gerrit the ONLY path into `main`) but provides no explicit rollback or emergency-restore plan if the Gerrit gate becomes unavailable or misconfigured after the cutover. The plan describes this as a live, gated step but says only 'the live apply gated on GitHub admin access' — no stated procedure to re-enable direct pushes or restore the old protection rule during a Gerrit outage.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria § B — WS7 (1bd2-1401-3a18-4031 / weedy-lath-abbey)
findings.suggested_fix: In the WS7 AC, add: 'A documented break-glass procedure exists (e.g., a GitHub Actions workflow or admin runbook) that re-enables direct-push to `main` and is tested before the Gerrit-only cutover goes live. The procedure is linked from `CONTRIBUTING.md`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit requirement (in WS6 AC or a new 'Secrets & Credentials' section) that all runtime secrets for the Gerrit voter (Gerrit HTTP password, LLM API key, GitHub token) are stored in and retrieved from a secrets manager (AWS SSM/Secrets Manager or equivalent) — never in plaintext config, committed env files, or IaC vars.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how runtime credentials for the Gerrit voter (Gerrit HTTP password, LLM API key, GitHub token for mirror-lock) are managed — no mention of SSM Parameter Store, Vault, or equivalent secrets manager for the new WS6 gate service.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS6 AC: 'Runtime credentials (Gerrit HTTP password, LLM API key) are sourced from AWS Secrets Manager / SSM Parameter Store; no secret appears in committed config, IaC vars, or environment files. Rotation does not require a redeploy.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Augment WS6-b's success criterion with a minimum quality bar for the healthy dogfood result (e.g., false-positive BLOCK rate below a threshold, voter abstain rate below a threshold) and a named gate before proceeding to WS7.
findings.finding: The dogfood success criterion (WS6-b) has no observable exit condition for the 'unhealthy' case: the plan states the goal as 'at least one real rebar change reviewed + gated end-to-end' but does not specify what a failed/unhealthy dogfood result looks like or what remediation step follows, leaving the E3 end-state proof incomplete for the failure path.
findings.location: WS6-b AC — 'LIVE dogfood: at least one real rebar change reviewed + gated end-to-end'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `sarif.from_sarif(trust_rebar_bag=False)` is a SHIPPED component (cite 8f6c or another closed epic) or NEW work in WS5, and if new, add an AC to WS5 specifying a test fixture that ingests a real gitleaks SARIF 2.1.0 output and asserts correct finding extraction (ruleId, location, severity mapping).
findings.finding: The plan asserts `sarif.from_sarif(trust_rebar_bag=False)` as an existing generic SARIF-ingest backend that serves both gitleaks and semgrep, but it is unclear whether this module is SHIPPED (part of the grounding oracle / detector registry from 8f6c) or is NEW work in WS5. If it is new work, its capability to correctly ingest gitleaks SARIF 2.1.0 output (which uses gitleaks-specific result properties) is an unverified assertion with no prior-art citation or test contract specified.
findings.location: Design — Decisions pinned section, secrets/security detector path bullet; WS5 AC
findings.suggested_fix: In the WS5 AC, add: 'If `sarif.from_sarif` is new, it must land as part of WS5 with a RED fixture test ingesting a gitleaks-format SARIF sample (with `fingerprints` + `partialFingerprints` fields) and a semgrep-format SARIF sample, asserting correct `Finding` extraction for both shapes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add cost/rate-limit controls to the gate config (`gates/code-review.yaml`) and WS6 AC: specify a per-change token budget cap, an API rate-limit back-off/retry strategy, and a CloudWatch (or equivalent) cost alarm threshold before WS7 fleet-wide enforcement.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no cost controls, rate limits, or quota considerations for running a four-to-seven-LLM-call review pipeline on every Gerrit change fleet-wide — an unbounded expense profile once WS7 makes this the mandatory gate.
findings.location: Design section (workflow shape) and WS6-b / WS7 ACs

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit, tested rollback procedure for WS7's GitHub cutover: document the break-glass step (e.g., re-enable direct-push branch protection via GitHub admin, or a Gerrit emergency bypass) and confirm it is exercised/documented before the live apply.
findings.finding: WS7 (GitHub mirror-lock cutover) has no described rollback path: once Gerrit is made the ONLY path into GitHub `main` and the legacy protection is removed, a fast undo requires GitHub admin access to re-enable direct push protection — but this is not documented as a tested, cheap rollback step. If the Gerrit voter misbehaves post-cutover, there is no described escape hatch.
findings.location: WS7 AC — 'make Gerrit the ONLY path into GitHub main (consume d251/S6 mirror-lock; remove the legacy protection)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the WS7 AC, add: 'Prior to applying the cutover, document and test a break-glass rollback: (1) the GitHub branch protection rule is archived (not deleted) so it can be re-enabled in <N minutes, (2) a named GitHub admin is designated to execute the rollback, (3) the rollback step is smoke-tested in a non-main branch before fleet-wide apply.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a break-glass rollback procedure to WS7 AC: document how to re-enable direct GitHub branch protection within a defined time bound (e.g., < 15 min) if Gerrit is unavailable post-cutover, and specify who holds the GitHub admin access required to execute it.
findings.finding: The plan provides no rollback mechanism or safety procedure for the WS7 cutover that removes legacy GitHub branch protection and makes Gerrit the sole path into `main` — if the Gerrit instance is unavailable or misconfigured post-cutover, merging to main becomes impossible with no documented recovery path.
findings.location: WS7 AC (1bd2-1401-3a18-4031) and 'Dependencies' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `depends_on WS4 (98ff)` to WS5 (bba6) and confirm WS5's forced-BLOCK consumer step is integrated into `produce_code_review_verdict` post-WS4 landing.
findings.finding: WS5 (bba6) must modify `produce_code_review_verdict` — a function built and owned by WS4 (98ff) — to insert the deterministic verdict-assembly forced-BLOCK short-circuit for detector abstain/positive results, but WS5's stated dependency is only WS3 (crow-knelt-fiber), not WS4. This is a real ordering violation: WS5 cannot implement its forced-BLOCK consumer step until WS4 has landed the function it modifies.
findings.location: child bba6-69b9-b9d4-4ac0 (WS5)
findings.suggested_fix: Add WS4 (98ff/side-haiku-dew) as an explicit dependency of WS5 (bba6) in the ticket. In the WS5 implementation plan, state explicitly that the forced-BLOCK short-circuit is a post-WS4 additive modification to `produce_code_review_verdict` (a new conditional block reading oracle results before returning the verdict).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align WS4 and WS6 on whether `INDETERMINATE` appears in the returned `code_review_verdict` dict: either add `'INDETERMINATE'` to the verdict schema (WS4) and confirm WS6's adapter handles it, or document that WS4 collapses INDETERMINATE→BLOCK internally and remove the INDETERMINATE row from WS6's adapter table (replacing it with coverage-gap logic inside WS4).
findings.finding: WS4 (98ff) defines the `code_review_verdict` schema with `verdict: 'PASS'|'BLOCK'` (two values only), but WS6 (4119) handles `INDETERMINATE` as a distinct, named verdict value from `produce_code_review_verdict` in its vote-mapping table and adapter logic. This is a conflicting assumption about whether `INDETERMINATE` surfaces in the returned verdict dict or is collapsed to `BLOCK` inside `produce_code_review_verdict` before returning.
findings.location: child 98ff-d3b8-d2f7-4a86 (WS4) and child 4119-1a4f-19ff-4cfa (WS6)
findings.suggested_fix: The cleanest fix: WS4 adds `'INDETERMINATE'` as a third allowed verdict value in `code_review_verdict.schema.json` (with `additionalProperties: true` already in place for forward-compat), and WS6's adapter table is correct as written. Update WS4's schema section accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement to WS6 AC: the code-review gate service must emit structured logs, an error-rate metric, and a latency metric (or CloudWatch equivalents) for the voter loop, the scanner invocations, and the LLM call chain, with at least one alarm threshold defined before WS6-b live dogfood begins.
findings.finding: The plan includes no observability specification (logging, metrics, or alarms) for the new AWS-hosted code-review gate service — the Gerrit voter process, the gitleaks/semgrep runner, and the LLM call chain have no stated monitoring, making production incidents invisible until a committer notices reviews are not being posted.
findings.location: WS6 AC (4119), WS6-b AC (0da5), and 'Current substrate / SHIPPED' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CI-enforced mechanism to keep `code-review-verify.md`'s verifier rules in sync with `VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD` — either generate the rule block from the constant at prompt-index regeneration time, or add a test that asserts the four rule texts in the prompt match `VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD` verbatim (preventing silent drift when the kernel adds or refines a rule).
findings.finding: The shipped `code-review-verify.md` manually inlines the VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD rule texts as prose rather than being mechanically generated from the kernel's `VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD` constant, while the plan's WS2 AC explicitly says 'a code verify preamble embeds `VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD`'. If `VERIFIER_RULES` in `verify.py` gains a new rule or refines existing text, `code-review-verify.md` silently diverges — the kernel and the verify prompt are no longer a single source.
findings.location: WS2 AC: 'A code verify preamble (code-review-verify.md) embeds VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD'; src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-verify.md lines 18-22 (hand-inlined rules); src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py:64-84 (VERIFIER_RULES / VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD definition)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test in the WS2/WS3 test suite: `assert all(text in prompt_body for name, text in VERIFIER_RULES)` where `prompt_body` is `get_prompt('code-review-verify').body`. This gives a failing CI check when the kernel's rules are updated but the prompt is not, without requiring a code-generation step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assign `CodeReviewBatchRunner` to either WS3 or WS4 (add it to that child's scope and ACs) so it is not an undocumented assumption between the two stories.
findings.finding: `CodeReviewBatchRunner` is cited in WS4 (98ff) as a WS3-shipped artifact ('code_review.batch_runner.CodeReviewBatchRunner(context: str) — the BatchRunner … WS3 already shipped (closed) the gate's runtime'), but WS3's (8855) scope, ACs, and deliverables make no mention of `CodeReviewBatchRunner`. This creates an unacknowledged deliverable gap: either WS3 must produce it (not stated) or WS4 must build it (not stated in WS4's scope either).
findings.location: child 98ff-d3b8-d2f7-4a86 (WS4), cross-referenced against child 8855-e5f9-05b4-4a04 (WS3)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `CodeReviewBatchRunner` (the BatchRunner subclass for the code-review gate) to WS3's scope and ACs, since WS3 owns the workflow runtime (`code-review.yaml` + scripted ops). Alternatively, move it to WS4's Slice 1 scope and remove the 'WS3 already shipped' claim for that class.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in WS3 whether `already_run` is sourced from the Round-A *triggers* output (intent) or the Round-A *batch execution* output (actual completion), and update the AC and test to assert the correct source — preferably the batch execution output for correctness under partial failure.
findings.finding: WS3 (8855) specifies that `overlay_union` reads `already_run` from 'the Round-A triggers step output', but the Round-A `batch` step (gated per-item by `when:=triggers`) may not execute all glob-triggered overlays (e.g. one overlay step errors or is skipped). Using the triggers-step output (what was *intended* to run) rather than the round_a-batch output (what *actually* ran) as `already_run` means a glob-triggered overlay that failed in Round-A could be re-dispatched in Round-B, violating the one-hop invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: Wire `already_run` to the round_a batch step's execution-output (the set of overlay ids that actually completed, with a fallback to the triggers set if the batch step exposes no per-item completion signal). Update WS3's AC to say 'wired to the round_a batch completion output' rather than 'Round-A triggers output'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace '~11 specialist reviewers' with an explicit named list (or minimum count + named taxonomy) of overlay IDs in the WS2 AC, so the fixed catalog enum is fully determined before implementation begins.
findings.finding: The overlay catalog size is stated as '~11 specialist reviewers' (approximate), leaving an executor unable to determine whether a catalog with fewer entries satisfies WS2's AC or whether additional specialists must be authored.
findings.location: WS2 AC: '(8e78-d515-3a98-4eba / make-rant-buoy) — overlay catalog (specialist reviewers with applies_to globs/content triggers)' and Design section: 'the ~11 specialist reviewers drafted in the catalog comments'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under WS2 listing the overlay IDs by name (e.g., `security`, `secrets`, `async`, `performance`, `api-surface`, `migrations`, `tests`, `dependencies`, `types`, `docs`, `config`) and mark any as OPTIONAL/v2 if not required for v1 closure, so the enum and the AC are unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Plan an explicit schema doc (inline YAML comments + a brief ADR entry or a `docs/code-review-gate.md` section) for `gates/code-review.yaml` covering: the escalation flow, cap semantics, threshold fields, and fail-closed posture — so the config is self-documenting and the design decisions are durably recorded.
findings.finding: The new `gates/code-review.yaml` config file introduces a novel architectural mechanism (two-round overlay escalation, cap N, per-criterion thresholds, fail-closed posture) that is not covered by the existing `docs/review-kernel.md` (which documents the kernel seam, not the gate config). No ADR or schema doc is explicitly planned for the config file's structure, semantics, or the escalation design decisions pinned in the plan (cap=3, one-hop, etc.).
findings.location: WS3 AC (8855-e5f9-05b4-4a04); Design section — 'Overlay activation'; Decisions pinned section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit commitment (in WS3 or WS4 AC) to create a persisted design doc or ADR (e.g., `docs/code-review-gate.md` or `docs/adr/NNN-two-round-escalation.md`) capturing the one-hop invariant, overlay cap rationale, Round-A/B separation, and verdict-side isolation — so the architectural intent survives after the epic closes.
findings.finding: The novel two-round base->overlay escalation architecture (overlay_union, one-hop cap, Round-B batch, verdict-side isolation) is not covered by an explicit ADR or persisted design-doc commitment beyond the epic text itself. Epics close; the rationale for WHY the architecture is one-hop, capped at N, and verdict-isolated will be invisible to future maintainers inheriting overlay_union + gates/code-review.yaml + the two-batch YAML structure.
findings.location: Design section — 'four-pass consumer + base->overlay escalation'; WS3 AC (crow-knelt-fiber); WS4 AC (side-haiku-dew)
findings.suggested_fix: In the WS3 AC add: 'Update `docs/code-review-gate.md` (create if absent) with the escalation mechanism design: one-hop invariant, cap=N rationale, overlay_union contract, Round-A/B separation, and verdict-isolation guarantee.' Reference this doc from `CLAUDE.md` under 'Architecture: Code Review Gate'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CI-checked fixture in WS5 that runs the pinned semgrep version against a synthetic rule file containing `metadata.rebar_envelope` and asserts the field survives in SARIF output — so a semgrep or ruleset upgrade that drops the field fails the build rather than silently degrading detector routing.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'semgrep passes `metadata.rebar_envelope` through verbatim' as a pinned capability fact grounded only in an offline derisk experiment (session_log a7e2-381c-6dc6-4d0c), but semgrep's handling of unknown `metadata` keys in community/vendored rule packs is not a stable documented guarantee — it is an observed behavior. Vendored rule updates (p/owasp-top-ten, p/cwe-top-25) could change metadata schema or strip unrecognised keys in a future semgrep release.
findings.location: Design — Decisions pinned section, 'Integration shape pinned by derisk experiment (Round-11)' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In WS5, include a `test_semgrep_envelope_passthrough.py` fixture that invokes semgrep with a minimal synthetic rule containing `metadata: {rebar_envelope: {severity: HIGH}}` and parses the SARIF output to assert `rebar_envelope` is present verbatim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation-cleanup step to WS4 (or WS7) that audits and updates every existing doc/ADR/README reference to `code_review.py` before or at the point of its deletion.
findings.finding: The plan retires and deletes `code_review.py` (the single-pass demo) but does not schedule an update to any existing documentation or references that name it. Any doc, README, ADR, or changelog entry that cites `code_review.py` will become a stale reference after WS4 closes.
findings.location: WS4 AC (98ff-d3b8-d2f7-4a86) and Context / 'Realignment' section

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the current export status of `aggregate_findings` / `normalize_finding` in WS4 and either (a) explicitly scope them as always-internal (non-breaking), or (b) add an expand-contract deprecation step if any consumer relies on them.
findings.finding: The plan says `aggregate_findings` / `normalize_finding` 'may survive as internal Pass-1 helpers' but does not confirm whether they are currently part of the public/exported surface. If they are referenced by external callers or documented as public APIs, their deletion or demotion is an unacknowledged breaking change with no migration note.
findings.location: Decisions pinned section — 'Re-export shim contract'; WS4 AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the coexistence/transition window for WS7: define whether open GitHub PRs must be drained/closed before cutover, and document the ordered sequence (enable Gerrit enforcement BEFORE removing GitHub protection, not after).
findings.finding: Old-and-new coexistence during the WS7 cutover moment is not specified: the plan does not describe what happens to in-flight GitHub PRs or direct pushes that are mid-flight when the legacy GitHub branch protection is removed and Gerrit becomes the sole gate.
findings.location: WS7 AC + 'Dependencies' section (d251/S6 mirror-lock)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS7: 'Cutover sequence: (1) confirm Gerrit LLM-Review label is enforcing on the target repo, (2) drain/close all open GitHub PRs, (3) THEN remove the legacy GitHub branch protection — never remove old protection before new enforcement is confirmed active.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the duplicate 'Integration shape pinned by derisk experiment' paragraph in 'Decisions pinned (2026-06)' — keep only one copy (the second, more complete one that includes the fail-CLOSED placement note).
findings.finding: The 'Integration shape pinned by derisk experiment (Round-11; session_log a7e2-381c-6dc6-4d0c)' block is copy-pasted verbatim twice inside the same 'Decisions pinned (2026-06)' section, creating a duplicate requirement block.
findings.location: Decisions pinned (2026-06) — secrets/security detector path subsection
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the first occurrence of the 'Integration shape pinned' paragraph (the one ending at '...Details on WS5 (bba6).') and retain only the second, which is identical but followed by the explicit fail-CLOSED placement rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Deduplicate the two 'WS6 staged rollout (Round-11)' bullets in 'Specifics pinned' — keep one canonical entry and remove the redundant second occurrence.
findings.finding: The 'WS6 staged rollout (Round-11)' bullet appears twice in the 'Specifics pinned (plan-review remediation 2026-06)' section with nearly identical text, constituting a near-duplicate requirement.
findings.location: Specifics pinned (plan-review remediation 2026-06) — WS6 staged rollout bullets
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the second 'WS6 staged rollout (Round-11)' bullet (the shorter one ending '(detail on WS6/4119)') and retain the first, fuller version.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider adding a thin end-to-end smoke-test milestone at WS1 or WS2 — even a stub verdict (base findings → kernel Pass-2/3/4 → typed result, no overlays) — so the kernel consumer seam is validated before WS2–WS3 build on top of it.
findings.finding: The first fully observable, end-to-end-verifiable vertical slice (a typed `code_review_verdict` returned from `produce_code_review_verdict`) does not land until WS4 — the fourth of seven workstreams. WS1–WS3 each deliver sub-components (assembler, catalog, ops) but none produce a checkable verdict. If an integration assumption (e.g., the kernel seam signature, the overlay union logic, or the gate-YAML schema) is wrong, the mismatch surfaces only at WS4, potentially invalidating WS1–WS3 work.
findings.location: Implementation sequencing (workstreams) — WS1 through WS4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS1's AC: 'a minimal end-to-end smoke test that passes the base reviewer's findings directly through the shipped `verify_findings` / `pass3_over_findings` / `coach` kernel calls and returns a stub `code_review_verdict` (no overlays, no detectors) — proving the consumer seam before WS2 catalog work begins.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
